evaluations.title: RAILGUN
evaluations.description: A privacy system for Ethereum using zk-SNARKs to shield ERC-20 tokens and ETH inside a smart contract, enabling private transfers and shielded DeFi interactions.

properties.name: Confidentiality
properties.notes: RAILGUN private (shielded) balances are built through a registry of UTXOs (unspent transaction outputs), similar to Bitcoin. The primary difference is that the RAILGUN UTXO list is encrypted with the sender and receiver’s keys, meaning that no outsider can view the contents of the UTXO.

properties.name: Scalable state
properties.notes: The RAILGUN system contains an efficient internal implementation of a batch-incremental Merkle Tree. Each shield interaction generates a new note which takes the form of a new Root/Leaf on the Merkle Tree. All tokens within the RAILGUN system share the same Merkle Tree. Every interaction that updates the state of the Merkle Tree will generate a new Merkle Root/Leaf that is hashed and secured by the zk-SNARK circuits.

properties.name: Client-side indexing
properties.notes: While generating the most up-to-date merkle tree, each commitment leaf (which represent a transaction) must be synced and decrypted in order to build a RAILGUN wallet’s private balance. This syncing process can take a few minutes. Once the sync is complete, UTXO token balances are aggregated into a user’s balance.

properties.name: Private state model
properties.notes: RAILGUN operates on a (U)TXO (unspent transaction output) model, (U) is in brackets as transaction outputs are completely secure from outside observers. Each UTXO is an encrypted note of a public key that establishes who can spend the underlying asset, amount, token ID, and a randomness field to maintain encryption.

properties.name: On-chain storage
properties.notes: RAILGUN protects this information by utilizing Private Balances, a confidential set of tokens and users where interactions appear to originate from. These Private Balances are known as an privacy set as, to an outside observer; transactions can be sent by anyone who has previously made a transaction.

properties.name: Access to DeFi
properties.value: Access to external, but limited choice of DeFi protocols
properties.notes: First, (1) an unshield is executed against the private balance, moving tokens temporarily into the Relay Adapt contract. Then, (2) a multi-call processes any number of contract calls against the balance. Finally, (3) the result is shielded back into the user’s private balance. This all occurs in a single block and one transaction.

properties.name: Programmability / Generality
properties.notes: Cross Contract calls are the primary method for RAILGUN to access external DeFi protocols and smart contracts. Any number of ordered contract calls can be wired together and executed against a private balance. These cross-contract interactions occur in the generalized RAILGUN Relay Adapt contract, which facilitates a number of serial multi-calls in a single block.

properties.name: Open source
properties.notes: The Railgun smart contracts are open source in Github and verified in Etherscan. There are multiple community-maintained wallets. You can find more information about the wallets in: https://railgun.org/wallets

properties.name: Cryptographic verifiability
properties.notes: Zero-Knowledge proofs are methods for one party, the prover, to mathematically satisfy another party, the verifier, the truth of information without revealing the originating details of the information.

properties.name: Point of enforcement
properties.notes: Private Proofs of Innocence checks begin when tokens enter the RAILGUN privacy system after a shield interaction.

properties.name: Escape hatch
properties.value: Can exit in a time period
properties.notes: With Private Proofs of Innocence, there is an Unshield-Only Standby Period, during which the only available action will be an unshield interaction to return tokens back to the original wallet. This Unshield-Only Standby Period is to give List Providers enough time to update their data such that bad actors are unable to hop addresses quickly to outrun data updates. As always, during this Unshield-Only Standby Period, users retain full control and custody over their tokens and can be unshielded back to the shielding address if needed urgently.

properties.name: Upgradeability
properties.notes: RAILGUN governance is decentralized, any wallet that locks RAIL can vote on and submit proposals to vote. For all governance stages, 1 RAIL locked is equal to 1 vote, snapshots of voting power are taken at each governance stage. Any change to the smart contracts (including the governance, treasury, and voting contracts) requires the passing of an on-chain proposal. The decentralized governance system is the only way in which upgrades to the RAILGUN protocol can be made.

properties.name: Implementation maturity
properties.notes: Railgun is one of the early private transfers protocols in Ethereum. You can check the deployment date of the Railgun Relay in the documentation link.

properties.name: Post-quantum secure
properties.notes: RAILGUN’s zk-SNARK circuits are proved using the Groth16 proof system, a pairing-based zk-SNARK design. Groth16 is based on Elliptic Curves Cryptography (ECC)

properties.name: Selective disclosure: viewing entity
properties.notes: . Viewing Keys enable the holder to decrypt the encoded interaction information but not send interactions. They merely scan the RAILGUN smart contract events to reveal what has been sent to users and what interactions users have sent.

properties.name: Selective disclosure: viewing control
properties.notes: Users can define the beginning and ending block for which the Viewing Key can observe in a scoped way. This is useful for auditability purposes, for example, a user can define the beginning and end of a tax year for their Viewing Key by block number.

properties.name: Type of compliance
properties.notes: Private Proofs of Innocence works at the balance and transaction level. Meaning, Private Proofs of Innocence proofs are generated after the initial shield transaction and apply to the tokens that are a part of that interaction. They are then carried forward for every following transaction within RAILGUN involving those tokens.

properties.name: Layer of enforcement
properties.notes: Private Proofs of Innocence is a tool that monitors tokens entering the RAILGUN smart contract to verify that they are not from a known list of transactions or actors deemed to be illegitimate. Upon a shield, a ZK proof is automatically created that proves that the tokens are not a part of a pre-set list of interactions and wallets

properties.name: Enforcement entities
properties.notes:
Private Proofs of Innocence List Providers:
Elliptic
ScamSniffer
PureFi
SlowMist
Chainalysis Sanctions Oracle

properties.name: Client-side proving
properties.notes: There is a distinct Groth16 prover for node/browser (snarkjs) and a different prover for mobile (native prover). Both provers generate Groth16 snarks using different assembly processes that are compatible with different platforms.

properties.name: Third-party inspectability
properties.notes:
With this knowledge of ZK proofs and zk-SNARKs, we can see how RAILGUN maintains privacy. Users can prove they can spend tokens without needing to share what those tokens are and how much they have of it. Broadcasters and the privacy set then enhance the data confidentiality for senders and recipients of interactions such that no identifiable information is compromised at any stage in the process.

Broadcasters are also unable to decrypt details about the interactions, nor can they change the details of an interaction as changing any information would lead to an invalid hash and be rejected by the system as an incorrect cryptographic proof.

properties.name: External network dependence
properties.notes: RAILGUN enables encrypted private transactions through Shielding. Once assets are shielded into the RAILGUN contract, there are several options: transfer tokens, use a cross-contract call, unshield tokens. In order to achieve full anonymity, RAILGUN uses a network of Broadcasters to submit transactions. This network is not obligatory, a user can directly interact with the smart contracts

properties.name: Censorship resistance
properties.notes:
Users and wallets are free to use whatever combination of List Providers they please, although some wallet providers will require a default List Provider. The ZK-cryptography generates an independent proof per Private Proofs of Innocence List Provider, which is verified and made available for further usage.

Users can use pools that do not require Private Proof of Innocence even though it is not the standard (default).

properties.name: Number of secrets
properties.notes: It requires an Ethereum wallet for public interactions and a private encryption key for Railgun interactions.

properties.name: Plausible deniability
properties.notes: The shield action sends ERC-20 tokens into the RAILGUN contract

properties.name: Asset privacy
properties.notes: Everybody can see that ERC-20 tokens are being sent to the Railgun contracts. In a private transfer, it is not possible to identify which asset is being transfered

properties.name: Anonymity
properties.notes: The output of RAILGUN circuits are UTXOs and Nullifiers, which is a hash generated from private keys that cannot be linked to a UTXO by anyone who is not a party to the transaction. The party holding the Spending Key is the only actor who can link which Nullifier as belonging to a corresponding UTXO.

evaluations.title: Tornado Cash
evaluations.description: A non-custodial, Ethereum-based mixer that uses zk-SNARKs to break the on-chain link between deposit and withdrawal addresses for fixed-denomination ETH and ERC-20 deposits.

properties.name: Confidentiality
properties.notes: When a user transfers assets into a pool (deposit), a private note is generated. This private note works as a private key or secret to access the assets. A user can have multiple private notes that are not related between each other therefore achieving balance confidentiality

properties.name: Anonymity
properties.notes: Tornado Cash achieves anonymity by breaking the on-chain link between source and destination addresses through "anonymity pools". These are immutable smart contracts that accepts fixed denominations of a currency, acting as what is known as a mix network.

properties.name: Asset privacy
properties.notes: The user sends a deposit transaction that sends their public assets to the Tornado Cash contracts. When the user withdraws the funds, the public assets are sent to the receiver address.

properties.name: Plausible deniability
properties.notes: You can see that users are depositing and withdrawing through the Tornado Cash contract

properties.name: On-chain gas cost: transfer
properties.notes: There are no internal transactions in classic Tornado Cash. The new Tornado Cash Nova allows shielded transfers.

properties.name: Number of secrets
properties.notes: You need an Ethereum wallet to send deposit and withdraw transactions. Also you need to store your private notes which give you access to withdraw funds in the future. You can configure a Note Account to store encrypted backups of privates notes on-chain.

properties.name: Censorship resistance
properties.notes: Tornado cash smart contracts are immutable and unstoppable. If the web UIs are taken down, users can build and setup their own in their local machine using their open-source code.

properties.name: External network dependence
properties.notes: There is a network of relayers which manage the withdrawal process. Users send their signed transactions to the relayer, the relayer pays for transaction fees and sends an on-chain transaction to be executed. The user receives the withdrawal amount minus the relayer fee.

properties.name: Escape hatch
properties.notes: Users can withdraw their funds from Tornado Cash at any time directly through the smart contracts. It is recommended to wait a time period and follow some tips to remain anonymous using Tornado Cash.

properties.name: Upgradeability
properties.notes: There is a decentralized governance structure that uses the TORN token as its voting token. It does not control the Tornado Cash Classic anonymity pools but it can control other parts of the protocol and its community tools.

properties.name: Client-side proving
properties.notes: zkSNARKs proofs are generated on the clients' devices without the need of any external service.

properties.name: Third-party inspectability
properties.notes: No third-party can link deposit to withdraws or obtain total user balances. This is achieved by saving on-chain the user encrypted generated data.

properties.name: Implementation maturity
properties.notes: The Tornado Cash Router contract was deployed on Feb 9th 2022.

properties.name: Post-quantum secure
properties.notes: It uses Pedersen hashing which is quantum vulnerable due to Shor's algorithm

properties.name: Layer of enforcement
properties.notes: There is no layer of enforce for compliance. Anyone can setup their own UI and relayer in order to interact with the deployed smart contracts

properties.name: Enforcement entities
properties.notes: External entities such as exchanges or dApps could potentially block access to addresses that have interacted with Tornado Cash

properties.name: Type of compliance
properties.notes: There is no compliance by default but users can generate selective disclosure reports linking deposit and withdraw in order to show it to the enforcement entity or regulator

properties.name: Point of enforcement
properties.notes: There is no compliance enforcement in Tornado Cash.

properties.name: Selective disclosure: viewing entity
properties.notes: Users can generate their own reports linking deposit and withdraw in order to share it with other third parties.

properties.name: Selective disclosure: viewing control
properties.notes: There is no view control, the compliance tool links the private note generated by the deposit action to the withdrawal transaction

properties.name: Cryptographic verifiability
properties.notes: It uses zkSNARKs, Merkle trees and Pedersen hashes as encryption functions

properties.name: Open source
properties.notes: All code (smart contracts, UI interfaces, relayers code) is public and open-source in their Github's organization. It is worth pointing out that in some point in the past these repositories were removed / hidden by Github.

properties.name: Scalable state
properties.notes: Each deposit generates encrypted data that is stored on a Merkle tree. As more transactions happens, more encrypted data will be stored in the Merkle tree as leaves.

properties.name: Client-side indexing
properties.notes: You need to manually input your private note in order to be able to withdraw funds. In case of using a Note Account, the encrypted backup is stored in a smart contract storage so it is easily accessible without scanning past transactions

properties.name: Private state model
properties.notes: Each deposit acts as a private note. In order to find the total balance, the user needs to sums up all its private notes.

properties.name: On-chain storage
properties.notes: The Tornado Cash smart contracts are deployed on-chain in Ethereum, BNB Chain, Polygon, Gnosis Chain, Avalanche, Optimism and Arbitrum One

properties.name: Access to DeFi
properties.notes: In order to interact with any DeFi protocol, the user needs to withdraw the funds from the Tornado Cash contracts.

properties.name: Programmability / Generality
properties.notes: Tornado Cash Classic is a set of tools to break the link between deposit and withdraws. Tornado Cash Nova allows for shielded private transfers between 2 users that have an account inside the Tornado Cash ecosystem. There has been no news to develop more programmable functionalities

evaluations.title: Privacy Pools
evaluations.description: A privacy protocol that extends Tornado Cash unlinkability concept with association set providers (ASPs), allowing users to prove membership in compliant subsets without revealing their identity.

properties.name: Confidentiality
properties.notes: Privacy pools inherits the technical design from Tornado Cash which is a note-based architecture. Therefore it is not possible to know the total balance in anonymity pools of a single user.

properties.name: Anonymity
properties.notes: Users can deposit assets into Privacy Pools and later withdraw them, either partially or fully, without creating an on-chain link between their deposit and withdrawal addresses.

properties.name: Asset privacy
properties.notes: Users have to deposit their public tokens to the anonymity pool and also they have to receive their public tokens from the pool into the receiver address

properties.name: Plausible deniability
properties.notes: Anyone can see that a user is interacting with Privacy Pools entrypoint contract

properties.name: Number of secrets
properties.notes: User needs an Ethereum wallet to interact with the smart contracts and also needs to store the nullifier and secret values which are locally generated in the user's device

properties.name: Censorship resistance
properties.notes: Users can choose in which anonymity pool deposit their funds depending on the Association Set Provider (ASP) configured on each pool. There could be cases where the pools does not have a strict ASP and anyone can use the protocol. But in practice users will use the anonymity pools with ASP in order to avoid linking their funds with illicit funds.

properties.name: External network dependence
properties.notes: The Association Set Provider needs to be running in other to allow deposit and withdraw function executions in the smart contracts

properties.name: Escape hatch
properties.notes: Ragequit is a safety mechanism that allows the original depositor to publicly withdraw their funds without needing ASP approval. This operation serves as a critical backup withdrawal method which ensures the ability to withdraw user funds when the label is not approved by the ASP or its approval was revoked

properties.name: Upgradeability
properties.notes: There is no Privacy Pools or 0xbow token to support decentralized governance. There is an owner reference in the Entrypoint smart contract

properties.name: Client-side proving
properties.notes: User constructs withdrawal parameters and later generates ZK proof of commitment ownership

properties.name: Third-party inspectability
properties.notes: No third-party can view the private information of the users.

properties.name: Implementation maturity
properties.notes: The Privacy Pools deposit contract was deployed on March 29th 2025

properties.name: Post-quantum secure
properties.notes: It uses zkSNARKs which are quantum vulnerable

properties.name: Layer of enforcement
properties.notes: The Association Set Provider blocks any suspicious withdrawal directly on the smart contracts. The user can still rage quit and withdraw to their original deposit address

properties.name: Enforcement entities
properties.notes: The Association Set Provider administrators

properties.name: Type of compliance
properties.notes: Depending on the ASP, there could be multiple compliance mechanism used in order to valid if a transaction is safe or not

properties.name: Point of enforcement
properties.notes: Withdrawals require valid ASP root

properties.name: Selective disclosure: viewing entity
properties.notes: Users could generate a report that links deposit and withdraw. There is no UI tool to do so but it is technically possible

properties.name: Selective disclosure: viewing control
properties.notes: There is no mention of viewing keys

properties.name: Cryptographic verifiability
properties.notes: It uses zkSNARK circuits written in Circom

properties.name: Open source
properties.notes: Smart contract and website code is open source. The ASP code cannot be found in the 0xbow (entity in charge of Privacy Pools) organization account

properties.name: Scalable state
properties.notes: All commitments are inserted in a merkle tree

properties.name: Client-side indexing
properties.notes: The private notes allow the users to withdraw their funds directly without the need to scan the blockchain for events related to the users' deposits

properties.name: Private state model
properties.notes: The on-chain commitments can be seen as private notes. In order to find the user's total balance, the user needs to sum the balance of each of his commitments (private notes)

properties.name: On-chain storage
properties.notes: Each commitment is saved in an on-chain Lean Incremental Merkle Tree (LeanIMT)

properties.name: Access to DeFi
properties.value: Access to external, but limited choice of DeFi protocols
properties.notes: Some tokens can earn yield privately while been idle on the anonymity pool

properties.name: Programmability / Generality
properties.notes: Privacy Pools v1 is similar to Tornado Cash. The main goal is to break linkability between deposit and withdraw function executions

evaluations.title: Tongo
evaluations.description: A confidential payment protocol on Starknet that uses ElGamal homomorphic encryption and zero-knowledge proofs to enable private ERC20 token transfers. Account balances are stored as encrypted ciphertexts on-chain, hiding amounts while keeping the protocol verifiable and supporting an optional auditor for regulatory compliance.

properties.name: Confidentiality
properties.notes: Tongo uses ElGamal homomorphic encryption to store account balances as encrypted ciphertexts. Each account maintains two balance types—current (usable) and pending (received)—both stored as ciphertexts, making it impossible for external observers to see any amount. Mathematical operations on balances are performed without ever decrypting them.

properties.name: Anonymity
properties.notes: Tongo uses an account-based model where each account is identified by a public key. Transfers are sent from one account's public key to another, meaning both the sender and receiver addresses are visible on-chain.

properties.name: Asset privacy
properties.notes: When funding a Tongo account, the user deposits a specific ERC20 token into the contract. The ERC20 token address is publicly visible during the funding operation, so the type of asset being shielded is observable on-chain.

properties.name: Plausible deniability
properties.notes: Tongo interactions are direct calls to the Tongo smart contract, making it publicly visible that users are interacting with the privacy protocol.

properties.name: Number of secrets
properties.notes: Users require a Starknet wallet to submit on-chain transactions (secret #1) and a separate Tongo account key pair used for ElGamal encryption and decryption of their private balances (secret #2). The Tongo account is created independently from the Starknet wallet.

properties.name: Censorship resistance
properties.notes: The docs talk about different compliance mechanisms that can be configured at deployment. It looks like Tongo takes the auditor path where a third party can examine transactions (real time monitoring, selective disclosure, retroactive investigations) but not censor a transaction or account. The advanced features talk about zero knowledge compliance features that include whitelist compliance which could be used to censor addresses on that specific Tongo instance.

properties.name: External network dependence
properties.notes: Tongo requires no trusted setup ceremony. The protocol's cryptography relies solely on the discrete logarithm assumption over the Stark curve, eliminating the need for any external trusted parties or multi-party computation ceremonies. All operations are self-contained within the Starknet smart contracts.

properties.name: Escape hatch
properties.notes: Tongo provides a ragequit mechanism that allows users to instantly withdraw their full encrypted balance back to the underlying ERC20 tokens at any time.

properties.name: Upgradeability
properties.notes: The Tongo smart contracts do not implement any proxy pattern or upgrade mechanism. Core parameters such as the conversion rate, bit size, and wrapped ERC20 address are immutable once deployed. The only administrative function available is rotating the auditor public key, which is restricted to the contract owner.

properties.name: Client-side proving
properties.notes: Tongo uses homomorphic encryption (ElGamal) and zero knowledge proofs to update the on-chain encrypted balances. The SDK contains prover functions that can be used on the user end devices

properties.name: Third-party inspectability
properties.notes: A global auditor (set by the contract owner) could decrypt encrypted balances for regulation and compliance. The auditor feature is activated at deployment and cannot be added after it.

properties.name: Implementation maturity
properties.notes: Tongo has been deployed on Starknet mainnet for less than one year. The implementation passed a security audit by ZKSECURITY.

properties.name: Post-quantum secure
properties.notes: Tongo's security relies on ElGamal encryption over elliptic curves. This scheme is vulnerable to quantum computing attacks via Shor's algorithm, making the protocol not post-quantum secure.

properties.name: Layer of enforcement
properties.notes: Compliance is enforced at the application layer through the optional auditor mechanism embedded in the Tongo smart contract or the whitelist compliance described in advanced features.

properties.name: Enforcement entities
properties.notes: The Tongo contract owner is the enforcement entity, responsible for configuring the auditor public key. The auditor role is a designated party (single key or threshold key encryption) that can examine transactions and potentially block addresses from using the protocol.

properties.name: Type of compliance
properties.notes: Tongo main compliance feature is the global auditor that can examine encrypted balances and transfer amounts. The advanced compliance features also allow for range compliance (transfer amounts less than N), velocity limits (maximum number of transfers per time period) and whitelist compliance (authorized addresses to use the protocol)

properties.name: Point of enforcement
properties.notes: The auditor can examine balances or amounts at all stages of the transaction lifecycle: deposits (funding), internal transfers, and withdrawals.

properties.name: Selective disclosure: viewing entity
properties.notes: Tongo supports two viewing entity types: the auditor can view transaction details without explicit user consent (involuntary third-party disclosure), and users can also voluntarily disclose their transaction history to any third party.

properties.name: Selective disclosure: viewing control
properties.notes: Selective disclosure controls (global auditor or user voluntary disclosure) is set at deployment.

properties.name: Cryptographic verifiability
properties.notes: Tongo uses ElGamal homomorphic encryption combined with zero-knowledge proofs to ensure that all operations (fund, transfer, withdrawal) are cryptographically verifiable. The protocol leverages Starknet's native elliptic curve operations for efficient on-chain verification.

properties.name: Open source
properties.notes: The Tongo protocol smart contracts (Cairo) and TypeScript SDK are publicly available on GitHub under the fatlabsxyz organization.

properties.name: Scalable state
properties.notes: Tongo uses an account-based model where each user's encrypted balance is stored as a mapping within the smart contract storage. New users add entries to the contract's account mapping, and the state grows proportionally to the number of accounts rather than to the number of transactions.

properties.name: Client-side indexing
properties.notes: Because Tongo uses an account-based model with balances stored directly as contract state, users can retrieve their encrypted balance directly from the contract without scanning past blockchain events or transactions.

properties.name: Private state model
properties.notes: Tongo maintains an account-based private state where each account holds an encrypted current balance (immediately spendable) and an encrypted pending balance (received but not yet rolled over). This mechanism prevents spam attacks to balances that could block senders from executing transfer transactions

properties.name: On-chain storage
properties.notes: All private state—including encrypted account balances (current and pending) and auditor configuration—is stored directly in the Tongo smart contract on Starknet.

properties.name: Access to DeFi
properties.notes: Tongo is a payment-focused protocol with no native DeFi integrations. The four core operations (fund, transfer, rollover, withdrawal) are all payment primitives. Users must withdraw from Tongo to interact with any external DeFi protocol.

properties.name: Programmability / Generality
properties.notes: Tongo is designed exclusively as a confidential payment system.

evaluations.title: Intmax
evaluations.description: A stateless zkRollup optimised for private payments, pushing most data off-chain so that on-chain costs are minimal while preserving user-side privacy through client-side proving.

properties.name: Confidentiality
properties.notes: Users construct their own transaction batches locally. These batches consist of arbitrary mappings from recipients to token amounts and are not revealed to aggregators. Each transaction batch is salted and hashed. Only these hashes are submitted to the aggregator, preserving privacy

properties.name: Anonymity
properties.notes: Sender and receiver are hidden when a transaction is submitted in the L2. The sender needs to share the ZK proof and required data to the receiver so he can update his balance in his local state (end-user device)

properties.name: Asset privacy
properties.notes: When users deposit assets into or withdraw assets from the Intmax network the token type is recorded on Ethereum L1. Inside the L2 the private transfers do not reveal the asset being transmitted

properties.name: Plausible deniability
properties.notes: Deposits from Ethereum to Intmax are submitted as standard L1 transactions to the Intmax Rollup contract, making it publicly visible on-chain that a user is entering the Intmax L2 network.

properties.name: Number of secrets
properties.notes: Users need their Ethereum wallet key to interact with the L1 contracts and a separate Intmax private key to authorise transfers within the network.

properties.name: Censorship resistance
properties.notes: The Intmax Liquidity contract is managed by a designated operator, which means access to deposits and withdrawals is gated by a centralised entity. This design introduces a single point of censorship at the L1 gateway that could block or delay user transactions. For example Intmax deposits and withdrawals were paused due to a ZK bug.

properties.name: External network dependence
properties.notes: Intmax relies on a network of external nodes (block builders, aggregators, and validity provers) to process and settle transactions. These roles are permissionless, meaning anyone can run the required services and participate in the network without needing approval from the core team.

properties.name: Escape hatch
properties.notes: Withdrawals from Intmax require the block builder to include the withdrawal in a batch and the validity proof to be submitted on-chain. Withdrawals from L2 to L1 require the user to submit a ZK proof of balance at a known commitment root. This root must exist in the contract’s on-chain history.

properties.name: Upgradeability
properties.notes: Intmax smart contracts are currently controlled by a single admin account with no on-chain governance or token-based voting mechanism. Until a decentralised governance model is introduced, all protocol upgrades depend on unilateral decisions by the core team.

properties.name: Client-side proving
properties.notes: ZK proofs for Intmax transactions are generated entirely on the user's device inside the supported wallet applications. This means no external prover service has access to private transaction data

properties.name: Third-party inspectability
properties.notes: Transaction contents are end-to-end encrypted and proven with ZK proofs, so neither the network operators nor any external observer can read private transfer data.

properties.name: Implementation maturity
properties.notes: Intmax launched on Ethereum mainnet in June 2025

properties.name: Post-quantum secure
properties.notes: Intmax uses Plonky2 for its ZK proofs. Plonky2 is considered post quantum secure because it is hash-based

properties.name: Layer of enforcement
properties.notes: AML compliance is enforced on the privacy mining feature designed to increase the anonymity set (deposit tokens into Intmax, wait a time period, withdraw to a different address). The L2 will block deposits coming from high-risk addresses and it will require a proof of innocence to allow deposits

properties.name: Enforcement entities
properties.notes: Compliance screening is performed by third-party AML service providers that supply the block-lists and risk-scoring data consumed by the Intmax protocol at deposit time.

properties.name: Type of compliance
properties.notes: Intmax applies Know Your Transaction (KYT) screening to incoming deposits and uses a Proof of Innocence (POI) model whereby users must demonstrate that their funds are not associated with sanctioned or illicit activity before being accepted into the network.

properties.name: Point of enforcement
properties.notes: AML and compliance checks are applied exclusively at the deposit stage when users bridge assets from Ethereum L1 into the Intmax network. Once funds are inside the network, subsequent transfers are not subject to additional compliance screening. Withdrawals are assumed to be clean if no bad actor has deposited into the L2.

properties.name: Selective disclosure: viewing entity
properties.notes: Intmax does not provide any viewing-key or selective-disclosure mechanism. Transaction history is accessible only through the user's own account.

properties.name: Selective disclosure: viewing control
properties.notes: There is no viewing-key system or programmable disclosure control in Intmax. Users cannot scope or share access to their transaction history with any external party.

properties.name: Cryptographic verifiability
properties.notes: Intmax uses ZK proofs to cryptographically guarantee the validity of every state transition (aka transfers). The architecture principles include formal verification of the circuits, ensuring that correctness is enforced by mathematics rather than by trusted intermediaries.

properties.name: Open source
properties.notes: The Intmax smart contracts, node software, and related tooling are publicly available in their open-source code repository, allowing anyone to inspect, audit, or contribute to the codebase.

properties.name: Scalable state
properties.notes: Intmax has a special L2 design similar to edge blockchains. Users are first-class agents who fully own and maintain their own state. Aggregators submit commitments on the L1 for global state coordination.

properties.name: Client-side indexing
properties.notes: Transactions are shared peer-to-peer from the sender to the recipient. The recipient needs to verify a ZK proof in order to update its own client-side state

properties.name: Private state model
properties.notes: Intmax represents private balances as a set of UTXOs. Each received transfer creates a new unspent output controlled by the recipient, and spending requires consuming existing UTXOs and creating new ones.

properties.name: On-chain storage
properties.notes: Intmax minimises on-chain footprint by storing only block roots and validity proofs on Ethereum L1. All transaction data is kept off-chain and propagated through the P2P network, with on-chain commitments serving as the source of truth for finality.

properties.name: Access to DeFi
properties.notes: Currently Intmax is designed as a private payment L2 network. At the moment the native DeFi ecosystem is small and Intmax does not give full access to Ethereum L1 DeFi ecosystem directly. Users must withdraw to Ethereum L1 to access any DeFi applications.

properties.name: Programmability / Generality
properties.notes: Intmax allows for integration into different applications and protocols. The ecosystem is still growing but it looks like Intmax can give programmability features to developers in order to build apps more complex than private transfers

evaluations.title: Fluidkey
evaluations.description: Fluidkey is a stealth address system on Ethereum that automatically generates a new Safe smart account for every incoming payment. It derives stealth addresses from the user's viewing key following ERC-5564, surfacing them through a static ENS name (username.fkey.id) that resolves to a fresh stealth address on every query, ensuring funds arrive at an address that no observer can link back to the recipient.

properties.name: Confidentiality
properties.notes: Individual transaction amounts are publicly visible on-chain as standard ETH or ERC-20 transfers. However, no external observer can aggregate a user's total balance because funds are spread across an unlimited number of unlinkable stealth addresses. The link between stealth addresses and the user's identity is hidden, making the effective total balance confidential while individual transfer amounts remain public.

properties.name: Anonymity
properties.notes: Stealth addresses provide unlinkability for the receiver: an outside observer sees funds sent to a fresh, never-before-used address and cannot determine who controls it. However, Fluidkey does not break transaction traceability for the sender — the origin of funds can be traced on-chain, so only the receiver's identity is protected.

properties.name: Asset privacy
properties.notes: Fluidkey is built on top of standard Ethereum token transfers. The token type (ETH, USDC, etc.) is always visible on-chain. Stealth addresses only obscure the link to the recipient's identity, not the asset being transferred.

properties.name: Plausible deniability
properties.notes: Because every incoming payment lands on a fresh stealth address, an external observer cannot prove that a specific stealth address belongs to a particular user without access to the viewing key. Fluidkey's Labels feature further allows users to manage funds across different identities (e.g., personal, freelance), giving additional deniability about the purpose or ownership of individual stealth accounts.

properties.name: Number of secrets
properties.notes: Each user has two derived key pairs: a spending key (used to control and sign stealth account transactions, never leaves the client) and a viewing key (used to derive stealth addresses). A BIP-32 derived node of the viewing key is shared with Fluidkey to enable server-side address generation and balance monitoring, while the spending key is re-derived locally on each session by signing a key-generation message.

properties.name: Censorship resistance
properties.notes: Stealth accounts are standard Safe smart accounts on public Ethereum networks. Any sender can transfer assets to a stealth address without interacting with Fluidkey's infrastructure. The open-source Fluidkey Stealth Account Kit allows anyone to independently derive stealth addresses and recover funds using only their signing key, without relying on Fluidkey servers.

properties.name: External network dependence
properties.notes: Fluidkey uses a proprietary ENS off-chain resolver to resolve user ENS names (username.fkey.id / username.fkey.eth) to fresh stealth addresses. This resolver is operated by Fluidkey and is not permissionless. If the off-chain resolver becomes unavailable, new stealth address lookups via ENS would fail, although existing stealth accounts and their funds remain fully accessible and recoverable independently. Anyone can generate and use stealth addresses without the need of an external dependency.

properties.name: Escape hatch
properties.notes: All stealth EOA private keys can be deterministically re-derived from the user's signing key using the open-source Fluidkey Stealth Account Kit. This means users can instantly sweep funds from any stealth account at any time without relying on Fluidkey's interface or servers. Smart contracts are only deployed on first use (counterfactual instantiation), so recovery remains possible even for undeployed accounts.

properties.name: Upgradeability
properties.notes: Fluidkey stealth accounts are Safe smart accounts, a battle-tested and immutable smart account implementation. The stealth address derivation follows the ERC-5564 standard and is implemented in an immutable open-source library. There is no admin key or upgrade mechanism that could alter the behaviour of deployed stealth accounts.

properties.name: Client-side proving
properties.notes: Fluidkey does not use zero-knowledge proofs. The stealth address derivation relies entirely on elliptic curve Diffie-Hellman (ECDH) and BIP-32 key derivation, both of which are lightweight cryptographic operations feasible on any device including mobile. No client-side ZK proving is required.

properties.name: Third-party inspectability
properties.notes: No third party can inspect a user's stealth account portfolio without the user's explicit consent. External observers see only unlinked stealth addresses on-chain. Fluidkey itself has read access to incoming balances and address generation through the shared viewing key node, but does not hold the user's spending key and cannot move funds.

properties.name: Implementation maturity
properties.notes: Fluidkey has been deployed on Ethereum mainnet and several L2 networks for more than two years. The core Stealth Account Kit was audited by Dedaub in May 2024, and an Earn Module audit was separately performed by Ackee Blockchain.

properties.name: Post-quantum secure
properties.notes: Fluidkey's stealth address derivation is based on elliptic curve Diffie-Hellman (ECDH) cryptography, which is vulnerable to Shor's algorithm on a sufficiently powerful quantum computer and is therefore not post-quantum secure.

properties.name: Layer of enforcement
properties.notes: Fluidkey does not enforce any AML/KYC or compliance rules at the protocol level. The stealth address system is permissionless — anyone can send to a stealth address without going through compliance checks. Users may voluntarily export their transaction history for personal compliance purposes.

properties.name: Enforcement entities
properties.notes: There are no on-chain or protocol-level compliance entities in Fluidkey. Transaction export for tax and compliance purposes is entirely optional and user-initiated.

properties.name: Type of compliance
properties.notes: Fluidkey supports selective disclosure through its transaction export feature, which allows users to voluntarily reveal their full transaction history to third parties such as tax authorities or auditors.

properties.name: Point of enforcement
properties.notes: There are no mandatory compliance enforcement points in the Fluidkey protocol. Transaction export is an optional user-initiated action with no protocol-level gate.

properties.name: Selective disclosure: viewing entity
properties.notes: Only the user can disclose their transaction history. Although Fluidkey has read access to incoming balances via the shared viewing key node, all voluntary disclosures to third parties are initiated and controlled by the user.

properties.name: Selective disclosure: viewing control
properties.notes: The disclosure mechanism is pre-defined through the user's viewing key structure. There is no programmable or scoped disclosure control — sharing the viewing key node shares all derivable stealth address data. The key sharing model is fixed at account setup time.

properties.name: Cryptographic verifiability
properties.notes: Fluidkey's stealth address scheme follows ERC-5564, which is based on ECDH. The derivation of each stealth address from the sender's ephemeral key and the recipient's viewing key is deterministic and cryptographically verifiable by anyone who holds the viewing key. This ensures that stealth account ownership can be proven without revealing the spending key.

properties.name: Open source
properties.notes: The Fluidkey Stealth Account Kit — containing all core cryptographic functions for deriving and recovering stealth accounts — is open source and publicly available on GitHub. Multiple independent recovery interfaces have been built on top of it.

properties.name: Scalable state
properties.notes: Fluidkey stealth accounts are counterfactually instantiated and each payment creates an independent smart account. There is no global shared state accumulation (no Merkle tree or registry that grows with usage). The system is effectively stateless at the protocol level — each stealth account is self-contained. There could be a potential issue on deploying independent smart accounts every time a transaction is performed.

properties.name: Client-side indexing
properties.notes: Because funds arrive at unlinkable stealth addresses, the user's wallet must continuously scan the blockchain for incoming transactions. Fluidkey performs this scanning server-side using the shared viewing key node and notifies users when funds arrive. Independent recovery requires scanning all ERC-5564 announcements to find transactions directed to the user's stealth addresses.

properties.name: Private state model
properties.notes: Each incoming payment creates a new stealth Smart Account at a fresh address. Each stealth account is an independent unit of funds — similar to a UTXO — owned by its controlling stealth EOA. There is no shared account balance; funds are distributed across multiple isolated stealth accounts that must be individually managed.

properties.name: On-chain storage
properties.notes: Each stealth account is a Safe smart account deployed on-chain. The contract holds the user's funds and the stealth EOA acts as the sole signer. Contracts are deployed lazily (counterfactual instantiation) — the smart contract is only deployed on-chain at first use, typically when the user wishes to move funds out.

properties.name: Access to DeFi
properties.notes: Fluidkey provides a native Earn feature that integrates with a curated selection of yield-generating protocols. Beyond this built-in integration, users can interact with Ethereum DeFi using their stealth accounts as standard Safe smart accounts, though the Fluidkey interface does not natively surface the full Ethereum DeFi ecosystem.

properties.name: Programmability / Generality
properties.notes: Fluidkey is designed primarily for private payments — receiving and sending funds to stealth addresses. The Fluidkey interface and protocol are focused exclusively on payment use cases, even though the underlying Safe smart accounts are theoretically programmable.

evaluations.title: Monero
evaluations.description: Monero is a alternative L1 blockchain focused exclusively on private transactions by default. It uses Ring signatures and stealth addresses under the hood to make the system secure, private and decentralized

properties.name: Confidentiality
properties.notes: Stealth addresses are used so there is no way to get a user's total balance because each transaction is send to a fresh stealth address. Ring Confidential Transactions (RingCT) hide transaction amounts using commitments and range proofs. More info about RingCT here: https://www.getmonero.org/resources/moneropedia/ringCT.html

properties.name: Anonymity
properties.notes: Ring signatures allows senders to hide themselves in a ring group of users. When a transaction is emitted, the sender signs the data using ring signatures (group signatures) so that the verification can guarantee the transaction is coming from a group member but does not know who

properties.name: Asset privacy
properties.notes: You can only send XMR in Monero

properties.name: Plausible deniability
properties.notes: Every transaction output has plausible deniability because the transaction uses a ring signature. All members of the group could deny sending that transaction and there will be no way to link the real sender to a specific group member.

properties.name: Number of secrets
properties.notes: There is a single spend key per account. There is also a viewing key that can be used to reveal the account's transactions.

properties.name: Censorship resistance
properties.notes: The privacy by-default feature hides senders and receivers interactions. This privacy property and the Proof of Work consensus mechanism make Monero censorship-resistant

properties.name: External network dependence
properties.notes: Monero uses Proof of Work which requires miners to join the network and validate transactions. The POoW algorithm is ASIC resistant, meaning that Monero miners must use consumer-grade hardware for an efficient mining process.

properties.name: Escape hatch
properties.notes: Users can withdraw funds from Monero to exchanges or other blockchains at any time. Exchanges could block Monero transactions depending on regulations and compliance.

properties.name: Upgradeability
properties.notes: The Monero community pushes changes and upgrades to the Monero Blockchain. These changes are implemented by different teams depending on the specifics (core team, wallet team, development team, etc)

properties.name: Client-side proving
properties.notes: Monero wallets compute all the required data on the client devices. There are multiple wallet options to chose from: GUI, CLI, mobile and hardware wallets.

properties.name: Third-party inspectability
properties.notes: Only the user has access to his balances and received transactions. The user can willingly share his viewing keys with any third-parties

properties.name: Implementation maturity
properties.notes: Monero was launched in April 2014. It was a fair, pre-announced launch of the CryptoNote reference code.

properties.name: Post-quantum secure
properties.notes: Monero cryptography is based on Elliptic Curve Cryptography therefore it is vulnerable to the Shor's algorithm.

properties.name: Layer of enforcement
properties.notes: There is no compliance enforcement mechanism in Monero blockchain. Some centralized exchanges can block Monero transactions due to national regulations or policies.

properties.name: Enforcement entities
properties.notes: There is no compliance enforcement mechanism in Monero blockchain.

properties.name: Type of compliance
properties.notes: The viewing keys were a selective disclosure mechanism for Monero users. According to their own documentation: outgoing transactions cannot be reliably viewed as of June 2017. Therefore, the balance of a Monero address as shown via a viewkey should not be relied upon.

properties.name: Point of enforcement
properties.notes: Some centralized exchanges do not accept Monero transactions.

properties.name: Selective disclosure: viewing entity
properties.notes: A user could share his viewing key with a specific third-party for compliance or tax purposes

properties.name: Selective disclosure: viewing control
properties.notes: There is no programable viewing control.

properties.name: Cryptographic verifiability
properties.notes: Monero uses a wide variety of cryptographic primitives for various use cases. Comparing to altcoins, Monero cryptography is considered conservative, sound and robust. Comparing to Bitcoin, Monero uses much more primitives, and some of them are more advanced, especially those related to privacy and Proof of Work.

properties.name: Scalable state
properties.notes: Monero has private key images as nullifiers to prevent double spending attempts.

properties.name: Client-side indexing
properties.notes: Stealth addresses make every transaction land on a fresh new address that is secretly controlled by a Monero account. In order for wallets to show the account balance, the application needs to be constantly scanning the network for transactions related to its corresponding stealth addresses

properties.name: Private state model
properties.notes: Every transaction on Monero has inputs (previously received transactions to a controlled stealth address) and outputs (transactions going to fresh new stealth addresses controlled by the receivers)

properties.name: On-chain storage
properties.notes: All data is stored on Monero blockchain

properties.name: Access to DeFi
properties.notes: There is no DeFi ecosystem in Monero due to its payment-only nature

properties.name: Programmability / Generality
properties.notes: Monero lives in a payments only blockchain with no programmability features
properties.url: Monero lives in a payments only blockchain with no programmability features
